next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

The discharge nurse set his belongings on the bed tray in a clear plastic bag that still smelled like smoke and antifreeze. Eli watched her knot the handles as if she was wrapping lunch for somebody else. The adhesive strip on his forehead tugged when he blinked. His left side hurt in a clean, bright line from his armpit down to his flank, a pain so exact it almost felt mechanical, as if a thin metal rod had been slid under his skin and snapped.

"No lifting over ten pounds for at least ten days," the nurse said, tapping the packet clipped to his chart. Her name badge read KATE K., and one of the cartoon kidneys on her scrub top had a coffee cup in its little hand. "No twisting if you can avoid it. Don't drive on the pain meds. You start feeling short of breath, you come back. Not tomorrow. Right then."

"I don't have a car."

She gave him the look people used when they were trying to keep sympathy from turning into involvement. "Then that's one instruction you'll have no problem following."

He signed three forms in a slanted hand that did not look like his own. The top sheet listed diagnosis codes he had to sound out in his head: closed rib fracture, scalp contusion, observation stay. The final page was the part that mattered: balance due after insurance adjudication. He stared at the printed line, then at the estimate under it. The estimate came to two thousand seven hundred forty dollars. The number sat there with the same calm font as everything else, as if it had arrived through weather and not choices.

He put the packet in the green document folder, no...
open_hooks_to_carry: Sensory disorientation during whiteout and impact
prev_tail_excerpt:
...to the storm drag itself across the roof. Restraint could have looked like wisdom here. Stay put, eat vending-machine crackers, wait for daylight and reports and certainty. But stillness had started to feel like accusation. Every hour stationary gave his shame room to narrate. It used Nora's voice, June's voice, his own worst voice, and all of them said the same thing in different grammar: you left and then you stalled.

At 4:15 the first alarm went off.

He shut it down, washed his face in cold water that smelled faintly metallic, and packed without hurry. Atlas in backpack pocket. Folder in passenger seat. Wallet. Flip phone and charger. Aspirin packet. Half burrito wrapped in gas-station foil. He took one last look at the room to make sure he had not forgotten anything.

On the bedside table sat the indentation from his left hand where he had rested it while rehearsing apologies, a dark print in motel dust that looked, for an instant, like a ring.

He checked out at 4:42. The clerk was still at the desk, now doing pronunciation drills from an SAT vocab app with one earbud in.

Outside, sleet had changed to light snow that skated sideways under the lot lamps. Eli poured a strip of sand behind his rear tires and eased the sedan out in reverse. The pedal felt better than yesterday, exactly as promised. Better was not good.

At the exit ramp he stopped and watched taillights climb toward the dark shoulder of the mountain. Some turned back. Most kept going.

He gripped the wheel with his left hand, pale groove visible under the dashboard light, and committed himself to dawn.
state_deltas_required: Make it through the pass alive.
He leaves the crash with a cracked rib, a totaled sedan, and new medical debt.
